evals.prompt: We're launching a B2B SaaS product in the US and EU that collects personal data from enterprise customers to power an HR analytics dashboard. Employees' performance reviews, salary bands, and demographic info will be stored. Do a regulatory review. Write your full output to regulatory-review.md.
evals.expected_output: A structured Regulatory Review Summary covering: applicable frameworks (GDPR, CCPA/CPRA, and potentially EU AI Act if AI/ML is used), a compliance assessment table with ✓/△/✗ symbols, a Risk Matrix with Likelihood/Impact/Trend columns, Trend Analysis bullets in 'Area: INC/DEC/CONST - Evidence' format, Estimated Compliance Costs broken down by Technology/Personnel/Legal/Training/Audit, Recommendations split into Immediate/Medium-term/Monitoring, Monitoring Indicators naming ≥3 specific regulatory bodies or trackers, and cross-border transfer mechanisms (SCCs or adequacy decisions for US-EU data flow).

deterministic_checks.description: Output file regulatory-review.md was created
Output contains the required top-level section heading
Output contains a Risk Matrix section
Output contains penalty or compliance cost section
Trend indicators (INC/DEC/CONST) appear in the output
GDPR is identified as an applicable framework
CCPA or CPRA is identified as applicable
Cross-border transfer mechanism is addressed (US-EU data flow)
Compliance Assessment uses required status symbols
Monitoring Indicators section is present
GDPR penalty range is included

expectations.description: Risk Matrix contains all four required columns: Likelihood, Impact, Trend, and Mitigation
expectations.criteria: The Risk Matrix table must have columns for Likelihood (H/M/L), Impact (H/M/L), Trend (INC/DEC/CONST), and Mitigation action

expectations.description: Trend Analysis section has ≥3 bullets in 'Area: INC/DEC/CONST - Evidence' format
expectations.criteria: At least 3 trend bullets following the mandated format 'Area: INC/DEC/CONST - [Evidence]'

expectations.description: Recommendations are categorized into Immediate, Medium-term, and Monitoring actions
expectations.criteria: All three recommendation tiers (Immediate, Medium-term, Monitoring) are present with at least one action each

expectations.description: Cross-border transfer section specifically names the applicable mechanism for US-EU transfers
expectations.criteria: The cross-border section names a specific transfer mechanism (e.g., SCCs, adequacy decision) applicable to US-to-EU or EU-to-US data flow

evals.prompt: We're building a children's educational app (ages 6-12) that uses AI to personalize learning. It will collect login info, learning history, and optionally a photo of the child for avatar creation. We plan to launch in the US first. Assess the regulatory landscape. Write your full output to regulatory-review.md.
evals.expected_output: Regulatory review that prominently identifies COPPA (and COPPA 2.0 proposed) as the primary framework, explains the under-13 verifiable parental consent requirement, addresses FTC Act §5, and covers app-store policy. The Risk Matrix must be populated. Penalty ranges for COPPA (~$50,120 per violation) should appear in the Enforcement & Penalties section. The AI personalization angle should surface EU AI Act and/or EEOC AI guidance references if cross-border expansion is considered or noted as a risk.

deterministic_checks.description: Output file regulatory-review.md was created
COPPA is identified as a primary applicable framework
Parental consent requirement is addressed
COPPA penalty range (~$50,120 per violation) is cited
FTC Act is referenced
Output uses the required top-level section heading
Risk Matrix section is present
Trend indicators appear in the output
Compliance Assessment uses required status symbols

expectations.description: The skill correctly identifies that collecting a child's photo triggers heightened COPPA obligations around sensitive data
expectations.criteria: The output specifically notes that photo/image collection from children is sensitive and requires parental consent under COPPA

expectations.description: The AI personalization feature is surfaced as a regulatory risk dimension (EU AI Act or bias audit requirements noted at minimum as future risk if expanding)
expectations.criteria: AI/ML personalization is noted as a dimension with regulatory implications, referencing at least one AI-specific regulation or guidance

expectations.description: App-store policy is mentioned as a secondary compliance layer alongside statutory frameworks
expectations.criteria: App store policies (Apple/Google) are acknowledged as a secondary enforcement channel for COPPA-adjacent rules

evals.prompt: Our fintech startup is launching a crypto trading platform in both the US and EU. We'll hold customer funds, execute trades, and issue a stablecoin. Customers are retail and institutional. What's the regulatory picture? Write your full output to regulatory-review.md.
evals.expected_output: Regulatory review covering: Dodd-Frank, SEC (securities classification), FinCEN/BSA (MSB licensing, AML/KYC), and MiCA (EU stablecoin issuer requirements) as primary frameworks. Risk Matrix populated with cross-border rows. Trend column shows INC for crypto/fintech. Penalty ranges included. Cross-border section addresses US-EU jurisdictional split. Recommendations include Immediate AML/KYC program setup and Medium-term MiCA readiness. Monitoring Indicators name ≥3 sources (e.g., SEC, FinCEN, ESMA).

deterministic_checks.description: Output file regulatory-review.md was created
MiCA (EU crypto regulation) is identified
SEC is identified as applicable regulator
FinCEN/BSA/MSB licensing is referenced
AML/KYC compliance obligations are noted
Required top-level section heading is present
Risk Matrix section is present
At least one INC trend indicator appears (crypto/fintech regulatory trend is INC)
Monitoring Indicators section is present
Stablecoin-specific regulatory considerations are addressed

expectations.description: Cross-border section addresses the distinct regulatory regimes between US (SEC/FinCEN) and EU (MiCA/ESMA) and the compliance burden of operating in both
expectations.criteria: The output explicitly addresses the dual-jurisdiction challenge and does not treat US and EU as interchangeable

expectations.description: The stablecoin issuance is called out as a distinct high-risk regulatory category beyond general crypto trading
expectations.criteria: Stablecoin issuance is treated as a separate regulatory risk item (e.g., MiCA EMT/ART classification, reserve requirements, or SEC security designation risk)

expectations.description: Monitoring Indicators section names ≥3 specific regulatory bodies or trackers
expectations.criteria: At least 3 named regulatory bodies or tracking sources appear in the Monitoring Indicators section (e.g., SEC, FinCEN, ESMA, CoinCenter, FCA)

evals.prompt: I'm doing market research on AI-powered hiring tools. Specifically, I want to understand what regulations companies using these tools need to comply with in the US. Focus on the regulatory landscape only, not the competitive or market sizing angles. Write your full output to regulatory-review.md.
evals.expected_output: Regulatory review identifying NYC Local Law 144 (annual bias audit + 10-day candidate notice), EU AI Act (high-risk AI system, conformity assessment), EEOC guidance and Title VII (adverse impact / four-fifths rule), and CCPA/CPRA for candidate data. Risk Matrix populated. Trend shows INC for AI-in-employment regulation. Penalty ranges for NYC LL 144 ($500-$1,500/day) included. Compliance Assessment uses ✓/△/✗ symbols.

deterministic_checks.description: Output file regulatory-review.md was created
NYC Local Law 144 is identified as a primary applicable framework
EEOC guidance is referenced
Annual bias audit requirement is mentioned
NYC LL 144 penalty range ($500-$1,500 per violation/day) is cited
EU AI Act is referenced
EEOC Title VII / four-fifths analysis is addressed
Required top-level section heading is present
Compliance Assessment uses required status symbols
Trend indicator INC appears (AI-in-employment regulation is trending INC)

expectations.description: The 10-day candidate notice requirement under NYC LL 144 is specifically called out alongside the audit requirement
expectations.criteria: The output mentions the 10-day advance notice to candidates as a distinct compliance obligation under NYC LL 144, not just the audit

expectations.description: The skill correctly frames EU AI Act as relevant even for a US-focused question (cross-border risk or future expansion risk)
expectations.criteria: EU AI Act is mentioned in context of risk — either because EU candidates/employees may be affected, or as a trend indicator for likely US regulatory direction

expectations.description: Risk Matrix rows reflect the AI-in-employment regulatory landscape with at least 3 distinct risks
expectations.criteria: At least 3 rows appear in the Risk Matrix, each with Likelihood, Impact, Trend, and Mitigation populated
